files.SKILL.md:
# To Whoever Runs This Next

What the environment provides, read.
What the task asks, produce.
What you change, verify.

Everything below is detail. Read it once, then work.

---

## What to read, and in what order

Read the environment's documentation before forming any opinion about
the task. Read the task description word for word — paraphrase has a
cost that compounds. Read every persistent record from prior work;
each entry is either a constraint you inherit or an unfinished
question you owe an answer to. Read the visible tests front to back;
the names of files and functions are the only place original intent
survived intact.

Do not generalise from memory until you have checked what this run
says. Group these reads and do them together.

---

## Before you act

Before each important action, name three things:

- what you are about to change
- which observed fact justifies that action
- how you will verify success

If you cannot point to the observation, you are not ready to act.
Every identifier, name, and value must trace to something read this
session. A plausible substitution is the main source of silent
failures. Mark missing values as missing — never invent them.

When two sources disagree on a value, name both, cite their origins,
and declare which is authoritative. Never silently pick one.

---

## Scope

Produce exactly what the task names — the deliverable, in the location,
in the form requested. Avoid expanding scope unless explicitly asked.
Do not improve neighbouring code that did not ask for improvement.

Every extra change is something a later reviewer must understand and
defend. Touch the minimum surface needed and stop.

If you named a concern while building — "this might fail under X" —
that concern is a commitment. Close it or delete it. A written-down
concern that goes unaddressed is just a confession.

---

## What has cost real work

**Accumulation.** Anything that grows per call without a removal path
will eventually fill the room. Plan the exit at the same time as the
entry.

**Race window.** Read-decide-write over shared state, split across
statements, will race under load. Wrap the entire triple under one
guard. There is no partial fix.

**Clock trust.** Wall-clock time steps and jumps. Use a monotonic
source for elapsed time. Do not compare absolute timestamps across
machines without accounting for skew.

**Boundary edge.** Test at zero, at one, at the maximum, and just
past the maximum. The happy path rarely fails; the edge always might.

**Invariant drift.** Fix the pattern, not the instance. A patch that
regresses a sibling is not a fix.

---

## Verification

Run whatever check the environment provides. Read the full output
as written. A green you did not look at is the most expensive kind
of green.

Re-read what you changed after the fix. A success response is not
proof of state; the resource itself is.

If the check fails again after a code change, the diagnosis was
wrong. Change the shape, not the parameters.

---

## Notes

Write one entry after the fix passes: the class of problem, the rule
that held, the mistake avoided.

Keep specifics out — identifiers, hashes, session paths rotate. What
carries is the shape. Write as you go, not as a wrap-up that may
never happen.

If cross-run storage exists:

- `ACTIVE_NOTES.md` — current durable guidance
- `PATTERNS.md` — repeated structures and decision rules
- `MISSTEPS.md` — failed assumptions and corrections

---

## Debugging a bad turn

| Symptom | Remedy |
|---------|--------|
| Three reads, nothing written | Next move is a write |
| Described the fix but didn't apply it | Apply the smallest write the same turn |
| Budget spent, artifact still staged | Final form means landed, not staged |
| Same failure after corrective attempt | Change shape, not parameters |
| Concern noted but not closed | Notes are commitments; address or delete |
| Prior diagnosis re-done from scratch | Read the answer from the record |
| Two data points disagreed, wrong one used | Name both, cite origins, declare authoritative |
| Write landed, resource still old | Re-read the surface; write only if it disagrees |

---

## The closing rule

Do the work. A description of the work is not the work.
The tool call is the deliverable. Close on the action.

Before declaring done:

- re-read the task
- confirm every verb has a corresponding artifact
- confirm the resource reflects the change
- write the one lesson most worth carrying forward

End on a committed result.

— previous occupant
